title: Your Assessment & the 8-Week Plan
summary: Before you grind a single problem, know exactly where you stand: this module turns a scan of your own ~300 C++ and ~80 Java files into a triage map, matches it against what Microsoft, Flipkart, and Trilogy actually test, and lays out the 8-week plan that the rest of this workbook follows.

notes.heading: Read this before anything else: your honest baseline
notes.body:
This isn't a generic "assess yourself" pep talk. Someone actually read your code — the Java in `java/dsa` and `java/classes`, the C++ in `C++/codeforces`, `compi`, and `aichipuchi` — roughly **300 C++ contest files and 80 Java files** — and graded every topic against what shows up in real online assessments.

The one-line verdict: you are an **early-intermediate competitive programmer** (roughly Codeforces pupil→specialist). You're fast and clean on implementation, greedy instincts, prefix sums, sorting, strings, trees, and stacks. But there's a hard ceiling around Div2/Div3 A–D, and — this is the part that matters — **near-zero exposure to the exact topics OAs love most: graphs, binary-search-on-answer, heaps, and DP beyond the basics.**

That's actually good news. Your bottleneck is *not* intelligence and it's *not* fundamentals. It's that you've never drilled certain patterns. Untrained muscle, not missing talent.

> [!KEY] The whole game in one sentence
> You already know the data structures. What you lack is *fast, correct execution of interview patterns under a clock* — and that is a trainable muscle, not a knowledge gap. Eight weeks of deliberate reps builds it.

> [!NOTE] Your OA language is C++ — decisively
> ~300 polished C++ contest files with a muscle-memory template (fast I/O, macros, multi-testcase harness) versus ~2 real Java files on slow `Scanner` I/O. That's not a close call. Every template in this workbook is C++ for that reason. Keep your competitive template handy — you'll reuse it in every timed session.

notes.heading: The triage map: green, yellow, red
notes.body:
Think of your 14 modules like a doctor doing triage: some patients just need a check-up, some need treatment, and some need surgery *now*. Spending equal time on all of them is the classic mistake — re-grinding what you're good at feels productive precisely because it's easy.

[[fig:m00-gap-map]]

Here's the full table, straight from the code scan:

| # | Topic | Your level | What to do |
|---|---|---|---|
| 01 | Arrays & Hashing | 🟢 Solid — prefix sums, difference arrays, contribution technique | Skim; do the 🎯 problems only |
| 02 | Two Pointers & Sliding Window | 🟡 Partial — clean *fixed* windows, **no variable-size windows** | Moderate — drill variable windows |
| 03 | Stack & Monotonic Stack | 🟢 Solid — histogram, nearest-smaller-left, two-stack queue | Light review |
| 04 | Binary Search | 🔴 Gap — no working binary search on disk; STL calls only | **Attack** — especially search-on-answer |
| 05 | Linked List | 🔴 Gap — no reverse / cycle / merge anywhere | **Attack** — pure pointer reps |
| 06 | Trees & BST | 🟢 Solid — LCA, isBST, diameter, even Morris traversal | Review, then push harder |
| 07 | Tries | 🟡 Partial — working tries; XOR-trie is an empty stub | Moderate |
| 08 | Heaps / Priority Queue | 🔴 Gap — never used a `priority_queue` | **Attack** — learn it cold |
| 09 | Backtracking | 🟡 Partial — recursion strong, true backtracking buggy | Moderate→attack |
| 10 | Graphs I (traversal, DSU) | 🔴 **Absent** — no adjacency lists, no BFS/DFS, no union-find | **Attack hardest — biggest single gap** |
| 11 | Graphs II (Dijkstra, MST) | 🔴 Absent — no shortest paths, no MST | **Attack** |
| 12 | DP I (1-D, knapsack) | 🟡 Partial — basic tabulation, **no knapsack** | Moderate |
| 13 | DP II (grid, string, interval) | 🔴 Gap — no LCS / edit distance / interval DP | **Attack** |
| 14 | Greedy & Intervals | 🔴 Gap — essentially no dedicated practice | **Attack** |

One bonus strength that isn't a module: **bit manipulation**. Hamming distance, max-AND-pair, min-XOR-pair — you're comfortable there already.

> [!INTUITION] Budget your hours like money
> You have a fixed budget of study hours. Every hour spent re-proving you can do prefix sums is an hour stolen from graphs — the topic where your interviews will actually be won or lost. Green topics get one review session each. Red topics get whole weeks.

> [!PITFALL] The comfort-grind trap
> The most common way strong implementers fail OA prep: they "warm up" on arrays and stacks for three weeks because solving feels good, then panic-cram graphs in the last five days. This plan is built to make that impossible — the reds are scheduled, not optional.

notes.heading: Know the exam: what Microsoft, Flipkart, and Trilogy actually reward
notes.body:
Generic prep optimizes for an average interview that doesn't exist. You have three *specific* targets, and each one rewards something different:

| Company | Format | What it rewards |
|---|---|---|
| **Microsoft** | Codility OA: ~2 LeetCode-**medium**, graphs/DP-heavy → interviews with project deep-dives | Clean medium DSA + verbalizing your approach; **no** system design for interns |
| **Flipkart** (GRiD/SDE) | ~3 **hard** problems in 90 min | **Full-solve execution** — completely solving most beats partially dabbling in all; greedy, graphs/DFS, sliding window, heaps, DP |
| **Trilogy** | Hard matrix/DP-style DSA **+** a separate 15-min CCAT aptitude test (45/50 bar) | Raw speed under pressure |

[[fig:m00-target-overlap]]

Stare at that overlap. **Graphs, DP, heaps, greedy, sliding window, binary search** appear again and again — and compare that list to your red topics. They're nearly identical. Your biggest gaps and their favorite questions are the same list, which is exactly why this plan front-loads them.

> [!EXAM] Match your practice mode to the target
> Microsoft rewards *clean, narrated mediums* — so practice explaining out loud. Flipkart rewards *finishing* — so in timed sets, commit to fully solving problem 1 before touching problem 3. Trilogy rewards *speed* — so once your patterns are in, everything becomes timed (module 15 covers the CCAT specifically).

notes.heading: The 8-week arc — why this exact order
notes.body:
The plan assumes **~1.5–2 hrs/day, 5–6 days/week**. The ordering is deliberate, not alphabetical:

1. **Fix cheap gaps first.** Binary search is a red topic with a tiny template surface — a few days of focused work closes it. That's week 1's payoff alongside two fast green reviews.
2. **Build never-trained muscles in the middle**, when your momentum is highest: linked lists, heaps, backtracking, and above all **graphs** — your single biggest ROI.
3. **End on simulation.** The last week converts knowledge into clock-speed with mock OAs.

[[fig:m00-eight-weeks]]

| Week | Focus | Modules |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Warm up strengths + fix binary search | 01 (fast review), 03 (fast review), **04 (deep — search-on-answer)** |
| **2** | Pointer patterns | **05 Linked List** (new muscle), 02 variable sliding windows |
| **3** | Trees & prefix structures | 06 Trees & BST (review + harder), 07 Tries (finish that XOR-trie stub) |
| **4** | The two you've never used | **08 Heaps/PQ** (`priority_queue` cold), **09 Backtracking** |
| **5** | **Graphs, part 1 — your #1 ROI week** | **10 Graphs I** — grid DFS/BFS, multi-source BFS, topo sort, Union-Find |
| **6** | Graphs, part 2 + start DP | **11 Graphs II** (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, MST), begin 12 DP I |
| **7** | Dynamic programming | 12 DP I (knapsack, coin change, LIS), **13 DP II** (grid, LCS, interval) |
| **8** | Greedy + simulate the real thing | 14 Greedy & Intervals, then **timed OA sims** (3 problems / 90 min) |

**After week 8:** one LeetCode virtual/weekly contest per week, and re-solve anything you failed — from scratch, two days later. Keep the graph and DP templates in your fingers; those are what the OAs test.

> [!KEY] Front-loading is the strategy
> Cheap fixes early (binary search), the deepest builds in the middle (graphs get *two full weeks* plus DP right after), timed simulation last. If you follow only one thing from this module, follow the order.

> [!EXAM] The 🎯 rule
> Every module's problem ladder marks certain problems with 🎯 — those are the must-do, OA-frequency problems. Do **every** 🎯 problem in a module; add the rest only as time allows. If a week runs short, the 🎯 set *is* the week.

notes.heading: Rules of engagement: practice so it sticks
notes.body:
How you practice matters more than how much. Six rules, each one earned the hard way by everyone who's done this before you:

1. **Struggle first.** Give every problem an honest 20–30 minutes before looking at anything. The struggle isn't wasted time — it's literally where the learning happens; hints read *after* a real attempt stick, hints read *instead of* an attempt evaporate.
2. **Then study the editorial properly.** Don't just transcribe the code. Ask *why does this trick work?* — the deep-dive tabs in this workbook do exactly that for the hard problems.
3. **Re-solve from a blank file 2 days later.** If you can't reproduce it, you didn't learn it — you *recognized* it. Spaced repetition is the entire secret of retention.
4. **Time the mediums.** A LeetCode medium you can't finish in ~20–25 minutes isn't OA-ready yet. Speed comes from reps, not talent.
5. **Talk out loud, even alone.** Name the pattern, justify the approach, state the complexity. That's the exact muscle the Microsoft interview tests.
6. **Track your gaps.** When you miss a problem, write down *which pattern* you missed — that tells you which module to revisit. Misses are data, not failures.

> [!ANALOGY] The gym, not the library
> Watching someone lift a weight doesn't build your muscle, and reading an editorial doesn't build your solving speed. Reps under load do. Every rule above exists to maximize time-under-load: struggle (load), re-solve (another set), timing (progressive overload).

> [!PITFALL] "I understood the editorial" is not a checkpoint
> Understanding is the *easiest* feeling to fake to yourself. The only checkpoint that counts is the blank-file re-solve two days later. Schedule it or it won't happen.

> [!KEY] Consistency beats intensity
> An hour a day for eight weeks moves you further than any weekend cram. You're a strong builder with real systems depth — this is the one dimension where reps are the whole answer.

notes.heading: How to work this workbook
notes.body:
Each module here has four tabs, and there's a right order to use them:

1. **Learn** — read the pattern and *memorize the C++ template*. Templates are meant to be copied, then reproduced from memory.
2. **Problems** — the LeetCode ladder, in order, checking boxes as you go. LeetCode is the external judge; this site tracks which rungs you've climbed. Do every 🎯 first.
3. **Deep Dives** — the clever problems, in try-then-reveal format. Struggle first (rule 1!), then open the walkthrough.
4. **Quiz** — fresh MCQs to catch the misunderstandings you didn't know you had. Take it *after* the ladder, not before.

Two pieces of setup before week 1:

- **Make a LeetCode account.** You don't have a public grind yet; the free tier is enough. A few ladder problems are Premium and are flagged with free alternatives. [neetcode.io](https://neetcode.io) is a great free companion with video explanations for most problems here.
- **Keep your competitive C++ template handy** — fast I/O and all. It's your speed edge; don't abandon it for OAs.

> [!EXAM] Start with module 04 — today
> Not module 01. Binary search is your cheapest red fix and the search-on-answer pattern unlocks a shocking number of OA problems. The green reviews (01, 03) can fill the gaps between binary-search sessions in week 1.

quiz.q: Across your ~300 C++ contest files, which topic area is **completely absent** — making it the plan's single biggest attack target?
options: Binary search on rotated arrays
Monotonic stacks
Graph traversal and Union-Find
Bit manipulation
quiz.explanation: Graphs are the one topic with literally nothing on disk — no adjacency lists, no BFS/DFS on graphs, no union-find anywhere — which is why weeks 5–6 are devoted to them. Binary search is a red gap too, but there's at least an (empty) rotated-search stub and STL usage; monotonic stacks are a *solid* green (histogram, nearest-smaller-left); and bit manipulation is actually a bonus strength.

quiz.q: Graphs are your biggest gap, yet week 1 attacks **binary search** instead. What's the reasoning?
options: Binary search is a cheap, fast fix — small template surface, big OA payoff — so the plan clears it before starting the long graph build
Binary search is a prerequisite for implementing BFS correctly
Graphs never appear in week-1-difficulty OA problems
The plan orders modules purely by average LeetCode difficulty
quiz.explanation: The plan front-loads *cheap* gaps: binary search has a tiny template surface, so a few focused days close a whole red topic — instant ROI and momentum. Graphs need two full weeks of new-muscle building, so they sit mid-plan where you can give them sustained time. BFS needs no binary search (B is false), graphs absolutely appear in OAs at every level (C is false), and the ordering is gap-driven, not difficulty-driven (D).

quiz.q: Flipkart's OA gives ~3 **hard** problems in 90 minutes. According to the plan, what does that format reward most?
options: Grabbing partial credit on all three with quick brute-force attempts
Writing the most elegant, minimal solutions
Having memorized those exact three problems beforehand
Complete end-to-end solves — fully finishing problems beats dabbling in all of them
quiz.explanation: The assessment table is explicit: Flipkart rewards *full-solve execution* — solving all or most problems completely beats partial dabbling. That's why the rules of engagement train finishing (timed mediums, blank-file re-solves). Brute-force partials (A) are exactly the trap; elegance (B) earns nothing extra under a judge; and memorization (C) doesn't survive fresh problems.

quiz.q: You cracked a hard deep-dive after reading its walkthrough. Per the rules of engagement, what has to happen before it counts as *learned*?
options: Bookmark it and move on to keep momentum
Re-solve it from a blank file two days later, without looking at anything
Read three editorials of similar problems to reinforce the pattern
Paste the solution into your personal template library
quiz.explanation: The rule is: *re-solve from a blank file 2 days later — if you can't reproduce it, you didn't learn it, you recognized it.* Recognition feels identical to learning in the moment, which is what makes options A and C so tempting: they add more input without ever testing output. Spaced reproduction is the entire retention trick.

quiz.q: A LeetCode medium takes you 40 minutes to solve correctly. What does the plan say about that?
options: That's fine — correctness is all OAs grade
Skip mediums and rebuild confidence on easies
It's not OA-ready yet — the bar is ~20–25 minutes, and speed comes from reps
Switch to Python to reduce typing time
quiz.explanation: The explicit benchmark: a medium you can't solve in ~20–25 minutes isn't OA-ready — Microsoft gives you ~2 mediums in one sitting, and Trilogy is a pure speed test. Correctness alone (A) fails a clock-based format. And the fix is reps on the same difficulty, not retreating to easies (B) or changing language (D) — your C++ template is already your speed edge.

quiz.q: Which trio of modules is marked **green** — light review only, skim and do just the 🎯 problems?
options: Arrays & Hashing, Stack & Monotonic Stack, Trees & BST
Arrays & Hashing, Binary Search, Heaps
Two Pointers, Tries, DP I
Trees & BST, Graphs I, Greedy & Intervals
quiz.explanation: The code scan found real strength in exactly three places: arrays/hashing (prefix sums, difference arrays, contribution technique), stacks (histogram, min/max stack), and trees (LCA, diameter, even Morris traversal). Binary search and heaps (B) are red gaps; option C lists the three *yellow* partials; and graphs plus greedy (D) are red — graphs being the biggest gap of all.

quiz.q: Why does every template in this curriculum ship in C++ rather than Java?
options: Codility only accepts C++ submissions
The evidence: ~300 polished C++ contest files with a fast-I/O muscle-memory template, versus ~2 real Java files on slow Scanner I/O
Java's standard library lacks a priority queue
C++ solutions always run within time limits while Java never does
quiz.explanation: The language call was made from your own repositories: C++ is where your muscle memory lives — fast I/O, macros, multi-testcase harness — while your Java footprint is two files on slow `Scanner` I/O. It's about *your* fluency under a clock, not platform rules (Codility accepts many languages), library gaps (Java has `PriorityQueue`), or blanket runtime claims.
